mission.title: Install and Configure Falco on Kubernetes
mission.description: Falco is a cloud native runtime security tool designed to detect and alert on abnormal behavior and potential security threats in real-time. Installing Falco enables you to enhance the security posture of your Kubernetes environment by monitoring system calls and integrating with your container runtime.

steps.title: Add the Falco Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Falco Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add falcosecurity https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Falco using Helm
steps.description:
Install Falco in the 'falco' namespace with the following command:
```bash
helm install falco falcosecurity/falco --namespace falco --create-namespace --version 0.43.0
```

steps.title: Verify the Falco installation
steps.description:
Check that the Falco pods are running successfully. Note that Falco runs as a DaemonSet, so you should see one pod per node.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n falco
```

steps.title: Verify Falco is detecting events
steps.description:
Check the Falco logs to verify it is running and detecting events:
```bash
kubectl logs -n falco -l app.kubernetes.io/name=falco --tail=50
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Falco will show a Falco pod running on each node in the 'falco' namespace (it runs as a DaemonSet). Falco does not expose a web UI — it logs security events to stdout and can forward them to external systems via Falcosidekick.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add falcosecurity https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Falco release from the 'falco' namespace using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall falco --namespace falco
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Falco. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd falcosecurity.org
kubectl delete pvc --all -n falco
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'falco' namespace and verify its removal with the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace falco
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of Falco. You can do this by exporting the current DaemonSet:
```bash
kubectl get daemonset falco -n falco -o yaml > falco-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade Falco to the latest version using the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade falco falcosecurity/falco --namespace falco --version 0.44.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Falco pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n falco
```

troubleshooting.title: Falco pod is not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Falco pod is in a 'CrashLoopBackOff' state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs -n falco -l app.kubernetes.io/name=falco
``` 
Look for any configuration issues or missing permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Falco alerts not triggering
troubleshooting.description:
If Falco is not generating alerts, ensure that the rules are correctly configured. Check the rules file:
```bash
kubectl exec -n falco -it $(kubectl get pods -n falco -l app.kubernetes.io/name=falco -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- cat /etc/falco/falco_rules.yaml
``` 
Make sure the rules are enabled and correctly defined.

troubleshooting.title: Falco events not appearing
troubleshooting.description:
If Falco events are not appearing, ensure the DaemonSet is running on all nodes:
```bash
kubectl get ds -n falco
kubectl get svc -n falco
``` 
If pods are not running, check the DaemonSet status and logs.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If the Falco pod is being killed due to out-of-memory (OOM) issues, consider increasing the memory limits via Helm values:
```bash
helm upgrade falco falcosecurity/falco --namespace falco --set resources.limits.memory=1Gi
``` 
Adjust the memory limit value accordingly.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
